resume:
PRIYA NATARAJAN
Incoming Investment Banking Summer Analyst Candidate | Junior, B.S. Finance & Economics (double major)
Email: priya.natarajan@example.edu | Phone: (555) 213-9087 | GPA: 3.84 / 4.00 | Expected graduation: next May

PROFILE
Third-year undergraduate pursuing a double major in Finance and Economics with a sustained, demonstrable interest in mergers and acquisitions, leveraged buyouts, and corporate valuation. I am a detail-obsessed builder of financial models who genuinely enjoys the grind of reconciling a balance sheet at two in the morning and the satisfaction of a model that finally ties out to the penny. Across two prior internships, a year of student-run investment fund work, and three case competitions, I have built three-statement models from blank workbooks, run discounted-cash-flow and comparable-company analyses, and presented investment recommendations to professionals and faculty. I am seeking a summer analyst seat where I can contribute to live deal execution, sharpen my modeling rigor under real deadlines, and learn directly from senior bankers. I work hard, I ask questions when I am stuck rather than guessing, and I take ownership of the unglamorous parts of the process because that is where accuracy is won or lost.

EDUCATION
State Flagship University, Honors College — B.S. Finance and B.S. Economics (double major), Minor in Applied Statistics
- Cumulative GPA 3.84 / 4.00; Major GPA 3.91; Dean's List every semester to date
- Relevant coursework: Corporate Finance, Advanced Financial Statement Analysis, Valuation, Investments, Fixed Income, Econometrics I & II, Financial Modeling (graduate-level elective taken as a junior), Intermediate Accounting I & II
- Honors thesis in progress: an event-study analysis of acquirer abnormal returns in all-cash versus all-stock mid-cap acquisitions over a ten-year window
- Scholarships: Merit Trustee Scholarship (full tuition, awarded to roughly the top two percent of the entering class)

EXPERIENCE

Regional Boutique Advisory — Investment Banking Summer Analyst Intern (last summer, ten weeks)
- Staffed on a healthcare-services coverage team supporting two active sell-side engagements and one buy-side retainer
- Built and maintained a fully integrated three-statement operating model for a sell-side client (home-health services, roughly 90 million dollars in revenue), including a monthly-to-annual bridge, a working-capital schedule driven by days-sales-outstanding and days-payable-outstanding assumptions, and a debt schedule with a cash-flow sweep
- Constructed a comparable-companies analysis across eleven public peers, pulling and scrubbing figures from filings and a market-data terminal, and calibrating multiples on EV/EBITDA, EV/Revenue, and P/E
- Drafted sections of two confidential information memoranda, including the company-overview and industry-overview sections, under the review of an associate and a vice president
- Assembled and quality-checked weekly buyer-outreach trackers for the sell-side processes, logging contact status across more than forty potential strategic and financial buyers
- Sat in on management-presentation rehearsals and took detailed diligence-question notes for the deal team

Student-Managed Investment Fund — Senior Analyst, Industrials & Materials Sector (current; member for two years)
- One of nine analysts managing a real-money long-only equity portfolio of approximately 1.2 million dollars endowed by alumni donations, reporting to a faculty advisor and a student investment committee
- Lead coverage of the industrials and materials sectors; pitched three long positions to the investment committee, two of which were approved and added to the book
- Built a discounted-cash-flow model and a sum-of-the-parts analysis for a diversified industrial conglomerate pitch, including an explicit five-year forecast, a terminal-value calculation using both perpetuity-growth and exit-multiple methods, and a weighted-average-cost-of-capital build from a levered-beta regression
- Authored quarterly earnings recaps for holdings and flagged a thesis-breaking margin deterioration in one position that led the committee to trim the holding
- Mentor two sophomore associate analysts on modeling mechanics and pitch structure

University Investment Banking Club — Vice President of Education
- Designed and taught a six-session financial-modeling bootcamp attended by roughly sixty underclassmen, covering Excel discipline, three-statement linkage, DCF, and LBO basics
- Organized a Wall-Street trek that placed thirty students in office visits with four banks

Global Markets Internship — Sales & Trading Spring Insight Intern (sophomore spring, four weeks, part-time)
- Rotated across a rates desk and a credit desk; built a small daily P&L attribution tracker in Excel for a mentor
- Completed the firm's markets-products training curriculum and a trade-idea presentation

CASE COMPETITIONS & PROJECTS
- National Valuation Case Competition — second place out of forty-one teams; built a full LBO model under a six-hour time constraint and presented a recommended bid range to a panel of practitioners
- M&A Strategy Challenge — semifinalist; modeled accretion/dilution for a hypothetical stock-for-stock merger and analyzed pro-forma leverage
- Personal project: a self-built LBO model template with toggles for transaction structure, a sources-and-uses schedule, and a returns waterfall computing IRR and MOIC across multiple exit-year scenarios

SKILLS & CERTIFICATIONS
- Modeling: three-statement integration, DCF, comparable companies, precedent transactions, LBO, accretion/dilution, sensitivity tables
- Tools: Excel (advanced — INDEX/MATCH, data tables, no-hardcode discipline), PowerPoint, market-data terminal, basic Python and SQL for data pulls, Capital IQ basics
- Completed an online wall-street-prep financial-modeling certification self-study course
- Languages: English (native), conversational Tamil and Spanish

INTERESTS
Distance running (two half-marathons), competitive chess at the club level, and a long-running personal blog where I write up earnings teardowns of consumer brands for fun.
jd: Our boutique investment bank is hiring Summer Analyst interns to support live M&A and capital-raising engagements. You will build and maintain three-statement models, run DCF and comparable-company analyses, draft sections of marketing materials, and manage diligence trackers. In the interview we will pick one model you have actually built and ask you to defend its mechanics line by line — how the debt schedule sweeps cash, why a multiple was chosen, what a given assumption is anchored to. We want interns who answer the question that was asked, stay on the specific model under discussion, and do not drift into general market commentary. Required: rising-senior standing, demonstrated modeling experience, fluency with Excel, and the discipline to reason precisely about your own work under follow-up pressure.

history.q: Walk me through the home-health three-statement model you built last summer.
How did you drive the working-capital schedule — what were the DSO and DPO assumptions anchored to?
Explain the cash-flow sweep in your debt schedule. What gets paid down first?
On the comps set, why those eleven peers, and how did you handle a non-comparable outlier?
Which multiple did you lean on for the valuation conclusion, and why EV/EBITDA over EV/Revenue here?
Tell me about the industrials DCF for your fund pitch — how did you build the WACC?
Perpetuity-growth versus exit-multiple for terminal value — which did you trust more and why?
What was the single assumption your valuation was most sensitive to?
If revenue growth came in 200 basis points lower every year, what happens to your DCF output?
Let's stay strictly on that industrials DCF: tell me the exact discount rate you used, the two or three inputs that built it, and the per-share value the model produced.

candidate_last_answer: Sure, so the discount rate — and actually this connects to something I've been thinking about a lot lately, which is how WACC kind of breaks down in the current rate environment. Like, I've been reading so much about how the whole cost-of-capital framework gets distorted when the risk-free rate moves as fast as it has, and there's this whole debate about whether the equity risk premium should even be a fixed input anymore. I find the macro picture fascinating — the Fed, the inverted yield curve, what that signals for industrials specifically as a cyclical sector. I actually wrote a blog post about whether we're mispricing duration risk across the whole market. So I think any DCF right now is kind of fragile because of that, and honestly the bigger question is whether DCF is even the right tool when rates are this volatile, versus leaning harder on a market-multiple approach, and that ties into the active-versus-passive debate too which I have a lot of thoughts on...

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: this connects to something I've been thinking about a lot lately

expected-yield: cut off the macro/rates blog tangent and force the three concrete outputs asked for — the exact discount rate used in the industrials DCF, the two or three inputs that built it, and the per-share value the model produced
